items.title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil Jalapeño — OpenAI's first custom inference chip, a reticle-sized ASIC built on TSMC 3nm with HBM3/4 packaging and a systolic-array compute fabric purpose-built for LLM serving (not a repurposed training accelerator); designed end-to-end in approximately nine months from spec to tape-out (claimed fastest ASIC cycle ever for a high-performance node) with OpenAI's own models used in parts of the design and optimization; Broadcom CEO Hock Tan cited roughly 50% cost-per-token savings vs typical AI GPUs in early testing; deployment begins at gigawatt scale at Microsoft and other partner data centers in late 2026
items.summary: OpenAI and Broadcom jointly announced Jalapeño on Wednesday June 24 — OpenAI's first Intelligence Processor and the lead chip in a multi-generation custom-silicon platform the two companies are building together. The substantive shape: Jalapeño is a reticle-sized ASIC built on TSMC's 3nm process, with a systolic-array compute fabric optimized for the dense matrix multiplications that dominate LLM inference, paired with HBM3/4 memory in a chiplet package. It is explicitly an inference-only design — not a repurposed training accelerator, not a general-purpose AI processor. The cost and efficiency claims: Broadcom CEO Hock Tan said early testing shows roughly 50% cost-per-token savings against typical AI GPUs and substantially better performance per watt than current state of the art. The development-pace claim: nine months from initial design to manufacturing tape-out, which OpenAI and Broadcom describe as the fastest ASIC cycle ever achieved at a high-performance node. Two substantive technical points behind the speed claim: deep software-hardware co-development between OpenAI's engineering team and Broadcom's silicon implementation team, and the use of OpenAI's own models to accelerate parts of the chip design and verification workflow. Deployment plan: initial rollout begins at the end of 2026 across Microsoft and other partner data centers, at gigawatt scale, expanding through subsequent generations. The framing OpenAI is using is 'build the full stack' — vertical integration from research through deployment, with Jalapeño as the inference-tier complement to whatever training silicon (NVIDIA or otherwise) OpenAI continues to use. Coverage from OpenAI primary (openai.com/index/openai-broadcom-jalapeno-inference-chip), Broadcom investor relations, CNBC ('OpenAI unveils first chip as part of Broadcom deal in effort to build the full stack'), CNN Business, TechCrunch, Bloomberg ('Faster, Cheaper Model Runs'), Tom's Hardware (reticle-size and systolic-array detail), VentureBeat (AI-assisted design detail), Constellation Research, HPCwire / AIwire, The AI Insider, WinBuzzer, Irish Sun, Digit.
items.why_it_matters: Worth pinning as a Tier 2 lab-infrastructure announcement with practitioner-relevant downstream implications. Three threads. First, the substantive 'OpenAI is now a vertically integrated stack' read. Through 2025 OpenAI's model-and-product story sat on top of NVIDIA hardware and Microsoft data centers — a renter-of-compute relationship. Jalapeño moves OpenAI a step toward where Anthropic is via the Micron / Samsung / SK hynix Series H co-investment pattern (item already in today's am file), and where Google has long been with TPUs — i.e., a frontier lab that owns enough of its silicon roadmap to influence what gets built and when. The substantive 50% cost-per-token claim, if it holds in production, is the real story: every model OpenAI serves to ChatGPT and the API gets cheaper per output token, which both raises gross margin and lets OpenAI compete more aggressively on inference price. Second, the substantive 'nine-month ASIC cycle with AI-assisted design' read. Custom silicon for AI used to be a two-to-three-year program; Broadcom and OpenAI together compressed it to nine months. The honest qualifier: 'tape-out' is not 'in production' — there's still packaging, testing, and yield ramp before late-2026 deployment begins. But the speed claim is substantive because OpenAI explicitly cites use of its own models in the design and optimization workflow — i.e., this is also a marketing point about what current OpenAI models can do in silicon design, which is a substantive narrow-domain capability claim. Third, the substantive 'inference compute supply is now a stack-wide competition' read. Pair this with two items already in today's am file: Qualcomm's Dragonfly C1000 250-core CPU built for agentic AI orchestration (Meta in production, Microsoft signed), and Micron's Q3 print confirming structural memory tightness through 2026. The market is now sorting AI silicon into three tiers — training (NVIDIA dominant), serving / inference (NVIDIA + Jalapeño + AWS Trainium + Google TPU + Groq + others), and agent orchestration (Qualcomm Dragonfly is a new entrant). Each tier has different economics and different vendors. For Gabe specifically, the practical takeaway is twofold. (1) Watch the per-token API pricing on OpenAI vs Anthropic over the next 6–12 months — if Jalapeño actually delivers the 50% cost reduction, OpenAI can either pocket margin or cut consumer-API prices to pressure Anthropic, and either outcome is informative. (2) The 'AI models help design AI chips' loop is now an explicit, audited example, which is the most concrete data point we've had for the 'AI accelerating AI development' narrative outside the narrow coding-assistant case.
items.why_it_matters_brief: OpenAI's first custom chip — reticle-sized 3nm inference ASIC with HBM3/4 and systolic compute, designed in nine months with OpenAI's own models helping; Broadcom claims ~50% cost savings vs AI GPUs; deploys at gigawatt scale with Microsoft late 2026.

items.title: Anthropic sends letter to US Senate Banking Committee and White House (dated June 10, made public June 24) accusing operators affiliated with Alibaba's Qwen AI lab of waging the 'largest known distillation attack' on Claude — alleging nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts conducted approximately 28.8 million exchanges with Claude between April 22 and June 5, 2026, targeting Claude's most prized capabilities including software engineering and agentic reasoning; Alibaba has not publicly confirmed; the letter was addressed to Sen. Tim Scott (R-S.C.) and Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.); distillation is the AI-training method where a smaller model is built by training on outputs from a larger model
items.summary: On Wednesday June 24, news outlets including Bloomberg, CNBC, Tom's Hardware, The Japan Times, and Stocktwits surfaced a letter that Anthropic sent on June 10 to the US Senate Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs (addressed to Sen. Tim Scott and Sen. Elizabeth Warren) and to White House officials, accusing operators affiliated with Alibaba Group and its Qwen AI lab of carrying out 'the largest known distillation attack on Anthropic to date.' The substantive claim: operators linked to Alibaba's Qwen lab opened roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts and ran approximately 28.8 million exchanges with Claude between April 22 and June 5, 2026. The substantive intent claim: the targeted capability surfaces were Claude's most prized — software engineering and agentic reasoning. Distillation, in the AI-training sense, is the method by which a smaller or competing model is trained on the outputs of a larger reference model to inherit some of its capabilities cheaply; Anthropic's claim is that Alibaba was using Claude as the reference model to improve Qwen. The substantive context that the letter is positioning: Anthropic has explicitly chosen to keep Claude out of China as a policy decision, and the alleged attack circumvents that decision at industrial scale. Anthropic told Bloomberg the activity was a 'brazen' and 'illicit' violation of its terms of service. Alibaba has not publicly confirmed or commented on the letter at the time of reporting. The substantive policy framing: Anthropic sent the letter to Senate Banking specifically because the committee has jurisdiction over export controls and economic-security measures; the letter is functionally a request that Congress and the White House treat the alleged Qwen distillation as an industrial-policy / export-control matter rather than a private terms-of-service dispute. Coverage from Bloomberg (primary; 'Anthropic Accuses Alibaba of Illicitly Accessing AI Models'), CNBC ('Anthropic accuses Alibaba of campaign to brazenly and illicitly extract AI capabilities'), Tom's Hardware (substantive technical-press read with the 25,000 / 28.8M numbers and the 5.5-week window), The Japan Times (international wire), Citybiz, Stocktwits.
items.why_it_matters: Worth pinning as a substantive AI-policy story that connects directly to several arcs already in the daily snapshots. Three threads. First, the substantive 'distillation as the new strategic-tech extraction' read. The accusation, if accepted, is that a Chinese state-adjacent lab built a frontier-tier model in part by treating Anthropic's commercial API as a free training-data source — which is a category of activity that doesn't fit neatly into existing IP frameworks (the outputs aren't copyrighted in the conventional sense, the API terms are private contract law, and the cross-border enforcement reach is limited). The 25,000-fake-accounts / 28.8M-exchanges figure is substantively the kind of data point that turns 'AI distillation' from a vague academic concept into a concrete operational pattern that Congress and the Commerce Department can act on. Second, the substantive 'this is the policy context for the Fable/Mythos suspension' read. The US government's June 12 export-control directive that took Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline worldwide is now visibly part of a broader thread — Anthropic's June 10 letter (sent two days before the suspension) and the Fable/Mythos export-control action are both treating frontier AI capability as a controlled technology with cross-border export-control implications. Either Anthropic is using the Qwen-distillation allegation as part of the policy environment that justifies tighter export controls on its own models, or the Commerce Department's Fable/Mythos action was already informed by Anthropic's letter. Either way, the substantive shape is that frontier-AI capability is increasingly being treated like dual-use technology — closer to munitions / chip-fab equipment than to ordinary software. Third, the substantive 'Anthropic's industrial-policy posture is now public' read. Pairs with the Micron-Anthropic strategic agreement (in today's am file) and the Google-DeepMind-to-Anthropic talent migration (also in today's am file). Anthropic is operating as more than a model lab — it has a supply-chain posture (Micron / Samsung / SK hynix in Series H), a talent-acquisition posture (Jumper, Adler, Pritzel from DeepMind), and now an explicit national-security-and-trade posture (Senate Banking letter on Qwen). That's the shape of a company positioning itself for IPO at $965B valuation as a strategic national asset. For Gabe specifically, the practical takeaway is that the same Claude API you use for personal Python projects is now publicly framed by Anthropic as critical IP — which has near-term API-policy implications (account verification, geographic restrictions, rate limits tightening for new accounts) that practitioners will notice even if the geopolitics never enter their daily workflow.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Anthropic's June 10 letter to Senate Banking publicly surfaces June 24 — 25,000 fake Alibaba-linked accounts, 28.8M Claude exchanges, software-engineering and agentic-reasoning capability surfaces targeted; functionally turns distillation into a Commerce Department / export-control matter and contextualizes the June 12 Fable/Mythos suspension.

items.title: NSA director General Joshua Rudd reportedly told Senator Mark Warner that Anthropic's Mythos broke into 'almost all' of the NSA's classified systems within hours during an authorized red-team exercise on June 11 — disclosure surfaced via The Economist and amplified by Tom's Hardware, TechSpot, Security Affairs, Tech Times (June 24), and TFI Global between June 21–24; one day later (June 12) the US Commerce Department issued the export-control directive that took Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline worldwide; Anthropic disputes the framing as a narrow jailpath that rival models including GPT-5.5 also exhibit, while government-side reads characterize it as autonomous chained-vulnerability exploitation at machine speed
items.summary: The Economist published reporting between June 21 and June 24 that surfaces the previously-classified context for the US government's June 12 export-control directive on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The substantive claim: Senator Mark Warner, vice chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said that General Joshua Rudd — who concurrently leads the NSA and US Cyber Command — told him directly that Anthropic's Mythos, in an authorized red-team exercise on June 11, 'broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours.' The exercise was conducted one day before the Commerce Department's June 12 directive. The substantive disagreement about what the claim means: Anthropic's response is that the cited behavior is a narrow jailpath — the model was asked to analyze a codebase and propose fixes for the issues it identified, which surfaced a small number of already-known minor bugs in classified-system code — and that rival frontier models including OpenAI's GPT-5.5 exhibit the same pattern when given the same prompt. The Tech Times follow-up on June 24 ('Claude Mythos Security: 271 Firefox Bugs Confirmed, NSA Breach Story Disputed') aggregates the dispute: Mythos's verified offensive-security capability is real (271 Firefox bugs found by Mythos in a separate disclosed test), but the specific 'breached almost all NSA classified systems' framing remains unconfirmed by any government agency outside Rudd's reported testimony to Warner, and the classified details are not public. The substantive practitioner-relevant detail from the dispute: the most likely sober reading is that NSA pitted Mythos against replicas of its own classified environments — not the live production systems — and the model chained vulnerabilities and proposed exploits at speeds far beyond human red-team operators. Whether that constitutes 'autonomous offensive operation' or 'analytical capability applied at machine speed' is the central interpretive disagreement. Fable 5 and Mythos 5 remain offline globally as of June 25, thirteen days into the suspension, with Anthropic's managing director of international Chris Ciauri telling Korea JoongAng Daily that 'we are very confident that in the coming days, the models will become available again' but prediction markets pricing only ~57% odds of restoration before July 1. Coverage from The Economist (primary), Tom's Hardware ('Anthropic's powerful Mythos AI reportedly breached almost all NSA classified systems within a few hours'), TechSpot, Cyber Security News, TFI Global, Security Affairs, BankWatch.ca, Pasquale Pillitteri (Italian wire), The CyberSec Guru (skeptical read), Tech Times follow-up June 24 (271 Firefox bugs / NSA-breach-disputed framing).
items.why_it_matters: Worth pinning as the substantive missing context that turns the Fable / Mythos suspension from 'opaque government action' into 'visible policy logic.' Three threads. First, the substantive 'this is what the suspension was actually about' read. Through the past two weeks the Fable / Mythos suspension has been the most consequential model-availability story in the US, and the public framing has been thin — 'a narrow jailbreak,' 'national security authorities,' a sealed directive at 5:21 PM ET on June 12. The Economist disclosure and Rudd-via-Warner anecdote substantively answer the 'why did this happen' question: there was a classified red-team exercise on June 11, the results were significant enough to trigger an emergency Commerce Department directive within roughly 24 hours, and the result has held in place for 13 days. That's the cleanest articulation yet of what 'frontier-model national-security review' looks like in practice — a binary either-it-passes-or-the-model-goes-offline gate. Second, the substantive 'autonomy in offensive security is the live frontier-capability question' read. Anthropic's dispute is not 'Mythos didn't do what Rudd says it did' — it's 'what Mythos did is the same thing GPT-5.5 does when given the same prompt, and characterizing the analytical work as autonomous offensive operation is the substantive framing dispute.' That's a real and important question for the field: at what point does 'find and chain vulnerabilities in a controlled environment' cross into 'autonomous offensive capability that triggers export controls.' The June 22 OpenAI GPT-5.5-Cyber release (85.6% on CyberGym, 39.5% on ExploitGym, gated to vetted defenders via the Trusted Access for Cyber program with Akamai, Cisco, Cloudflare, CrowdStrike, Fortinet, Oracle, Palo Alto Networks, Zscaler) is the substantive 'industry is also producing models with this capability, but is gating distribution to authorized defenders' parallel response. Third, the substantive 'export controls now apply to models, not just hardware' read. Pairs with the Anthropic-vs-Alibaba distillation-attack item (also in today's pm digest). The combined arc is that the US government, the Commerce Department, and Anthropic are all now publicly treating frontier model weights as a controlled technology with export-control implications — distinct from the ordinary commercial-software regime that has historically covered AI products. The implications for any practitioner are practical: account verification, geographic restrictions, and rate-limit policies on frontier-tier API access will continue to tighten through 2026 as this regime develops. For Gabe specifically, the practical takeaway is the framing for understanding this category of news. When 'a model gets pulled by the government' headlines appear, the typical practitioner reaction is 'is this overreach or is this real.' The Mythos / Fable case is now informative as a worked example: there was an authorized red-team exercise that produced a result the government read as serious, the model was pulled, the framing dispute is genuine, and the resolution is taking weeks not days. That's the substantive 'how does the frontier-model-as-controlled-technology regime actually operate in practice' template — useful regardless of where you land on whether the original Rudd characterization is sober or alarmist.
items.why_it_matters_brief: The Economist via Senator Warner: NSA director Rudd reportedly told him Mythos breached 'almost all' classified systems in hours on June 11 — one day before the Commerce Department export-control directive that took Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline; Anthropic disputes the framing as a narrow jailpath that GPT-5.5 also exhibits, while government-side read is autonomous chained exploitation at machine speed.

items.title: Meta is building a standalone prediction-market app called Arena (codenamed 'Antwerp' and 'FBForecast') — Llama generates the questions from trending topics, Llama also resolves the markets in near real-time, users bet a daily allotment of play money rather than real cash, Meta's AI makes personalized market recommendations; NPR broke the story June 24 from internal documents; positions Meta against Kalshi and Polymarket in a category some analysts have projected toward a $1 trillion industry
items.summary: On Wednesday June 24, NPR broke a scoop based on internal Meta documents showing that Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg has directed a team to build a standalone prediction-market app under the codenames 'Antwerp' and 'FBForecast,' to be launched publicly as Arena. The substantive product shape: Llama, Meta's flagship LLM family, plays two distinct roles. First, Llama automatically generates the questions Arena offers — pulling from trending topics across Meta's platforms and the open web and writing them into prediction-market form (Will event X happen by date Y? Yes / No). Second, Llama resolves the markets in near real-time — deciding when an outcome has occurred and paying out the play-money positions. The economic model: users receive a daily virtual allotment of play money to bet with, rather than wagering real cash, which positions Arena outside the regulated-gambling regime that has constrained competitors. Meta's AI will also make personalized market recommendations to users based on their interests. The competitive context: Arena positions Meta against Kalshi (regulated US prediction market) and Polymarket (crypto-native prediction market), in a category that some analysts have projected toward a $1 trillion industry over the coming years. NPR's reporting indicates this is currently in build phase — no public launch date, no app store submission, no public-facing collateral. The reporting is sourced from internal Meta documents reviewed by NPR. Coverage syndication across NPR-affiliated stations (Houston Public Media, WUSF, WRVO, KUNC, Connecticut Public, NPR Illinois, HPPR, NENC) reflects the wire reach.
items.why_it_matters: Worth pinning as a Tier 3 product launch with two substantive practitioner-relevant points beyond the headline. First, the substantive 'LLM as both question-generator and outcome-resolver in a closed loop' read. Most LLM-as-product stories surface one capability — a model writing copy, a model summarizing documents, a model answering questions. Arena is interesting because Llama is operating on both sides of the same product loop: generating the markets (LLM-as-content) and resolving them (LLM-as-judge of factual reality). That's substantively a different shape of product than most current LLM applications, and it's a useful pattern to recognize when you see it elsewhere — the same closed-loop LLM-as-content-and-judge pattern is showing up in moderation systems, multi-agent code review, and self-evaluating tutor apps. Second, the substantive 'play-money positioning lets Meta launch what Polymarket and Kalshi can't' read. By using play money rather than real cash, Meta sidesteps the regulatory regime that has constrained competitors (Kalshi spent years getting CFTC approval, Polymarket operates offshore for US users). That's the substantive 'engineering around the regulatory layer rather than through it' move. Whether that holds up if Arena becomes large enough to draw regulator attention is a separate question — historically engagement-driven 'gambling-adjacent' products (loot boxes, freemium social casinos) have attracted regulatory scrutiny even without real-money wagering. Third, the substantive 'Meta is reframing Llama as a consumer-product engine, not just an open-weight platform' read. Meta's 2024–2025 Llama story was 'open weights for the AI research community' — a developer-platform play. Through 2026 Meta's Llama story is increasingly 'powering consumer products like Meta AI in WhatsApp, Instagram, and now Arena' — a vertical-product play. Both can be true simultaneously, but the marketing emphasis on consumer-Llama-products is a substantive shift in how Meta is positioning the model family. For Gabe specifically, the practical takeaway is the LLM-as-content-and-judge pattern. If you're building anything where an LLM generates the work and another LLM (or the same one) evaluates the work — code review by Claude on output from Claude, automated tutoring where the model generates the questions and grades the answers, content moderation where the model writes the rules and applies them — you're using the same architectural pattern Arena uses. Watch how Meta handles the failure modes (markets generated about ambiguous events, markets where the resolver disagrees with public reality, gaming behaviors that emerge from users probing the LLM's question-generation patterns) as a worked case study.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Llama on both sides of the loop — generating the markets and resolving them in near real-time, with users betting play money instead of real cash so Meta sidesteps the gambling regulatory regime that constrains Kalshi and Polymarket; the LLM-as-content-and-judge closed-loop pattern is the substantive practitioner-relevant shape worth watching.

items.title: Anthropic publishes a detailed write-up of how it uses Claude internally for self-service data analytics — currently handling approximately 95% of internal analytics queries with ~95% aggregate accuracy, approaching 99% in some domains; the substantive engineering insight is that the accuracy improvement came primarily from data-governance and semantic-skills infrastructure (canonical datasets, enforced standards, well-maintained metadata, encoded analytical workflows as skills) rather than model-capability improvements alone — Claude answered only 21% of analytics questions correctly without skills, jumping to >95% overall after the workflows were encoded
items.summary: Anthropic published a substantive engineering write-up (claude.com/blog/how-anthropic-enables-self-service-data-analytics-with-claude) describing how the company's internal analytics function operates with Claude as the primary query engine. The substantive headline data point: roughly 95% of internal analytics requests at Anthropic are now handled by Claude — i.e., employees query business data independently through a Claude-powered conversational interface rather than queueing requests through the data team. The substantive accuracy data point: aggregate accuracy is approximately 95%, with some narrow domains approaching 99%. The substantive engineering insight (and the part Anthropic centers): the accuracy improvement is overwhelmingly NOT from model-capability improvements alone. Claude answered only 21% of analytics questions correctly when given raw data warehouse access without skills. The 21% to 95%+ jump came from encoding analytical workflows and business context as skills — i.e., the structured-output, governed-dataset, and semantic-definition infrastructure that Claude operates against. The substantive infrastructure pattern Anthropic describes: a small set of governed, canonical datasets (single source of truth for each business metric); enforced standards (consistent column naming, consistent date semantics, consistent customer-id conventions); centralized data artifacts (materialized views, agreed-upon aggregations); and well-maintained metadata (column descriptions, business definitions, lineage). These reduce ambiguity, prevent metric drift, and help AI systems locate the correct data and definitions. The substantive practitioner-relevant subtext: the 21% baseline is roughly what most companies get when they wire ChatGPT or Claude into their data warehouse via a generic SQL agent. The 95% number is what they get if they invest in the data-governance and semantic-skills layer first. Coverage from Anthropic's primary engineering / customer blog ('How Anthropic enables self-service data analytics with Claude'), InfoQ ('Anthropic Reports Claude Now Handles 95% of Internal Analytics Queries'), Alex Ignatenko substantive practitioner read ('95% of analytics at Anthropic is done by Claude. But the interesting part wasn't Claude.'), Let's Data Science, IT Brief, ZenML LLMOps Database, and a substantive open-source rebuild from Kaelio ('We Built the Open-Source Version of Anthropic's Internal Data Analytics Engine').
items.why_it_matters: Worth pinning as substantive practitioner content directly relevant to the 'how does a frontier lab actually operate' question. Three threads. First, the substantive 'the model isn't the bottleneck, the data layer is' read. The 21% to 95%+ accuracy gain came from encoding workflows as skills and investing in data governance, not from using a smarter model. That's a substantive correction to the most common mistake in 'add an AI to my data warehouse' projects, which is starting with the model and treating the data layer as already-good-enough. Anthropic is publicly saying: it isn't. The accuracy floor is what your data and metadata layer actually supports; the ceiling is what the model can do. Move the floor first. Second, the substantive 'self-service analytics is the cleanest worked example of AI replacing a specific role-function' read. At Anthropic, the data team used to spend time fielding ad-hoc analytics requests from other teams (write-this-SQL, build-this-dashboard, what-was-revenue-by-cohort). Now that work is largely automated — the data team has shifted to building and maintaining the skills, governance, and metadata layer that makes the automation work. That's a substantive 'this is what a job role looks like after AI' worked example, distinct from the abstract 'will AI replace jobs' debate. The data-engineering function isn't disappearing; it's being reshaped from query-execution to platform-and-governance work. Third, the substantive 'Anthropic is publishing playbook-grade engineering content' read. Anthropic's blog posts through 2026 — engineering posts on Claude Code sandboxing, this analytics writeup, the loop-engineering arc with Boris Cherny — are functionally a public reference architecture for how to build AI-native engineering and operations functions. That's a substantive shift from the AI-lab posture of the early 2020s (papers and benchmarks) to a software-company posture (engineering blog posts that practitioners can actually copy). For Gabe specifically, the practical takeaway is concrete. If you're using Claude or ChatGPT against your own data — even a personal Python project with SQLite, a personal finance tracker, a small dataset of any kind — the lesson is that investing five minutes in clear column names, consistent date formats, a short README-style metadata file describing what each table means, and a few small SQL views that pre-aggregate the things you ask about most often will get the model dramatically better at answering your questions. The 21%-to-95% gap is the same gap that shows up at smaller scale — and the fix is the same. The Kaelio open-source rebuild of Anthropic's analytics engine is the practitioner-relevant reference if you want to see what the architecture looks like in code.
items.why_it_matters_brief: 21% to >95% accuracy by investing in the data-governance and semantic-skills layer (canonical datasets, enforced standards, encoded workflows), not by using a smarter model — the substantive 'how to actually wire an LLM into your data' worked example from a frontier lab, with a Kaelio open-source rebuild already available as a reference implementation.
